metadata.file_name: 97. Chen et al. (2023). Using learners’ problem‑solving processes in computer‑based assessments for enhanced learner modeling: A deep learning approach.pdf
metadata.title: Using learners’ problem‑solving processes in computer‑based assessments for enhanced learner modeling: A deep learning approach

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses a real-world learner log dataset from PSLC DataShop (“Lab study 2012 (cleanedLogs)”) rather than an ILSA/PISA-style nationally representative survey. The dataset consists of 74 learners interacting with a web-based tutoring system on fraction problems, and the authors preprocess and split sequences into 20-timestep subsequences for model training/testing. The manuscript does not mention any complex survey design, sampling weights, or replicate-weight procedures (e.g., BRR/jackknife), and the modeling is performed with deep learning architectures (LSTM, attention, DNN) that are not described as incorporating survey weights. Therefore, weighting is treated as not applicable/omitted for this convenience log dataset.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: This paper does not analyze ILSA cognitive achievement scores with plausible values (PVs). The dependent variable is binary item-response correctness (correct vs incorrect) at each timestep derived from tutoring-system transactions, and the model is trained directly on logged process sequences (actions and time durations). Because the study is based on a constructed log dataset and does not report any PV-based imputation/deletion strategy, missing-data handling for the ML training data is not documented in the provided text, representing a reporting gap for how incomplete sequences/transactions were treated.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used the PSLC DataShop dataset “Lab study 2012 (cleanedLogs)” from a web-based tutoring system solving fraction problems. Preprocessing included deleting system-produced and/or non-timestamped transactions; treating hint-related actions as intermediate actions; concatenating action labels with learner selections to make actions differentiable; fixing maximum action and time sequence length at six (since >90% of items used ≤6 actions); and splitting long learner-item interaction sequences (typically >200 timesteps) into multiple 20-timestep subsequences. The final modeling dataset comprised 866 item sequences, 32 unique items, and 15 unique skills, with training using the selected latent skill model “KC (DefaultFewer_corrected)”.

main_findings.dataset_used: PSLC DataShop Lab study 2012 (cleanedLogs) — fraction tutoring (KC latent skill model)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Full model vs DKT and variants (Table 1). Training ratio 0.7: ACC 0.7225, AUC 0.7400, MAE 0.3580, RMSE 0.4254. Training ratio 0.5: ACC 0.7160, AUC 0.7323, MAE 0.3578, RMSE 0.4300. Training ratio 0.3: ACC 0.7126, AUC 0.7259, MAE 0.3616, RMSE 0.4330. Attention variant slightly outperforms LSTM variant; model without process-data learning is consistently worse than full model.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PSLC DataShop Lab study 2012 (cleanedLogs) fraction tutoring the study leveraged problem-solving action sequences, action time durations, learner/item embeddings, and the KC latent skill model to predict next-step item response correctness, finding that the full deep learning model achieved higher ACC/AUC and lower MAE/RMSE than DKT and a variant without process-data learning (e.g., at training ratio 0.7: ACC 0.7225 vs 0.7037 for DKT; AUC 0.7400 vs 0.7157). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study proposes a deep learning learner-modeling framework that sequentially predicts learners’ next item-response correctness using both product information (previous correctness) and process information (logged action sequences and associated time durations) from PSLC DataShop “Lab study 2012 (cleanedLogs)”. Across training/test partition ratios (0.3/0.5/0.7), the full model consistently outperformed the DKT baseline and a model variant that excluded process-data learning, improving both classification (ACC, AUC) and regression-style error metrics (MAE, RMSE). For example, at a 0.7 training ratio, the full model reached ACC=0.7225 and AUC=0.7400 with MAE=0.3580 and RMSE=0.4254, while DKT had ACC=0.7037 and AUC=0.7157. The attention variant slightly outperformed the LSTM-only variant. The paper does not report any survey weighting (appropriate for this log dataset) and does not document missing-data handling for incomplete sequences, which limits transparency.
